title: The Little Door
summary: Mara hears strange sounds in her new home at night.
coverScene: a child's attic bedroom with a tiny mysterious low door in the corner and an old pocket watch glowing faintly

sentences.text: Mara moves to an old house.
She moves there with her mother and father.
The house is outside the town.
It is big, old, and very quiet.
Mara gets the room at the top.
In her room there is a little door.
It is small and near the floor.
It does not open.
"It is for old things," says her father.
"Old houses have little doors like this."
One night, Mara wakes up.
It is three in the morning.
Sounds come from the little door.
Three small sounds.
Then three more.
Then the house is quiet again.
Mara tells her mother and father.
They hear nothing at night.
"You are dreaming," they say.
But every night it is the same.
Three sounds at three in the morning.
One day, her father opens the little door.
He works on it for an hour.
There are old things in there.
Old paper, old books, and an old watch.
The watch does not work.
Its hands stand at three.
Mara puts the watch on her table.
That night she wakes up again.
Three sounds come from the little door.
But the watch is not in there now.
Mara looks at her table.
The watch is not on the table.
She opens the little door slowly.
The watch is inside again.
And now it works.
Tik. Tik. Tik.
Mara looks at it for a long time.
"This is your home," she says.
She closes the little door.
Now the nights are quiet.
The sounds do not come back.
But sometimes, at three in the morning,
the little door says: tik.
And Mara says: "Good night."

options: Outside the town
In the town center
Near her school
At three in the morning
At nine at night
In the day

quiz.q: What is inside the little door?
options: An old watch
A little cat
New shoes

sentences.text: Last month, Mara moved to an old house with her parents.
The house stood alone outside the town, between tall old trees.
It was big and quiet, and the floors talked when you walked.
Mara got the room at the top of the house.
She liked it, but it felt a little strange.
In the corner of her room there was a little door.
It was half a meter tall and near the floor.
It did not open, and there was no key.
"Old houses have little doors for old things," her father said.
"Do not think about it too much."
One night, Mara woke up at three in the morning.
Small sounds were coming from the little door.
Three little sounds, then a wait, then three more.
It sounded like a small hand on wood.
Then the house went quiet again.
In the morning, she told her parents everything.
They listened, but they heard nothing at night themselves.
"New house, new dreams," her mother said kindly.
But the sounds came back every night at the same time.
Mara started to sleep with the light on.
She did not like that side of the room any more.
On Saturday, her father opened the little door with his tools.
It took him almost an hour.
Inside there were old papers, old books, and an old watch.
The watch was not working.
Its hands stood at exactly three o'clock.
"You see? Only old things," her father smiled.
Mara cleaned the watch and put it on her table.
That night, she woke up at three again.
The three sounds were coming from the little door.
But the door was empty now, she thought.
She looked at her table in the half dark.
The watch was not there.
The room felt very big and very still.
Her heart went fast, but she got up.
She opened the little door very slowly.
The watch was inside again, in its old place.
And now it was working: tik... tik.
Mara looked at it for a long time.
Then she said quietly: "All right. This is your home."
She closed the little door and went back to bed.
After that night, the sounds never came back.
The house is quiet now, and Mara sleeps well.
But sometimes, at exactly three in the morning,
one small "tik" comes from the little door.
And Mara, half in her dreams, says: "Good night."

quiz.q: Why did the little door not open at first?
options: It was closed and there was no key
It was too heavy for Mara
Her father did not allow it

quiz.q: What did the sounds sound like?
options: A small hand on wood
Water in the walls
A cat at the window

quiz.q: What happened after Mara said 'This is your home'?
options: The sounds never came back
The watch stopped forever
The family left the house

sentences.text: When Mara's family moved out of the city, they chose an old house outside town.
It stood alone at the end of a long road, surrounded by tall dark trees.
The floors sang under their feet, and the doors closed with heavy voices.
Mara chose the room at the top because of its view of the hills.
She loved the room immediately, though something about it felt older than the house itself.
In the corner, half hidden behind her bed, there was a little door.
It reached her knees, and its wood was darker than the wall around it.
There was a small lock, but no key anywhere in the house.
"Every old house has a door like that," her father said without looking up.
"They kept winter things in them, or shoes, or forgotten letters."
Mara nodded, but she moved her bed a little further from the corner.
The first sounds came on her third night in the house.
She woke suddenly and looked at her phone: it was exactly three in the morning.
From the corner came three soft knocks, patient and slow.
A pause followed, long enough for her to doubt her ears.
Then three more knocks, a little softer, like a question repeated politely.
After that, the house returned to its deep country silence.
Her parents listened to her story with kind, tired faces.
They had heard nothing, and the house had been checked before they bought it.
"Old wood moves at night when the air gets cold," her mother explained.
It was a good explanation, and Mara wanted to believe it.
But cold wood does not knock three times, wait, and knock three times again.
The sounds returned every night that week, always at three.
On Saturday, her father finally opened the little door with his tools.
Behind it they found a low, deep space filled with the past.
There were yellow newspapers, children's books, and a wooden box of photographs.
At the very back, wrapped in a piece of cloth, lay an old pocket watch.
It had stopped long ago, and its thin hands pointed exactly at three.
"You see?" her father said. "Nothing lives here except dust."
Nobody in the family knew whose watch it had been.
Mara cleaned the watch carefully and placed it on her table by the window.
It was beautiful in a sad way, like something waiting to be needed again.
She wondered who had carried it, and why it had stopped at that hour.
That night she woke at three, as she had known she would.
The three knocks came from the corner, patient as always.
But the little door stood open and empty; she had left it that way.
She turned to her table, and her breath stopped: the watch was gone.
She crossed the room slowly, telling her legs to be brave.
Inside the little space, in its exact old place, lay the pocket watch.
It was working now, filling the dark with a soft tik, tik, tik.
Mara knelt there for a long moment, listening to it breathe.
"All right," she whispered at last. "I understand. This is your home."
She closed the little door gently, like closing the cover of a book.
The knocking never returned after that night.
The house feels warmer now, the way houses do when an argument ends.
Her parents believe the sounds were only the house settling, and she lets them.
Mara has stopped sleeping with the light on.
But sometimes, at exactly three in the morning, a single tik comes from the corner.
And Mara, without opening her eyes, says quietly: "Good night."

quiz.q: Why did Mara choose the top room?
options: Because of its view of the hills
Because it was the biggest room
Because it had a little door

quiz.q: Why did Mara reject her mother's explanation?
options: Cold wood does not knock in a repeated pattern
The house was new, not old
Her mother had heard the sounds too

quiz.q: What did Mara find when she opened the little door at night?
options: The watch back in its old place, working
A small animal living inside
A hidden staircase going down

sentences.text: The house was cheaper than it should have been, which Mara's parents called luck.
It stood alone at the end of a country road, guarded by trees that had outgrown their garden.
Inside, every floor board had its own voice, and every door closed like a quiet opinion.
Mara claimed the top room, drawn by the wide view and the sloping ceiling.
It was perfect, she decided, except for the feeling that the room had already belonged to someone for a very long time.
The little door sat in the corner, half hidden by the shadow of her bed.
It was knee-high, made of wood so dark it seemed burnt, with a small lock and no key.
Her father explained that old houses were full of such spaces, built for winter boots and forgotten things.
His voice was calm, but Mara noticed that he tried the lock twice before leaving the room.
That evening she moved her bed to the opposite wall, telling herself it was about the light.
On her third night, Mara was pulled out of sleep as if a hand had been laid on her shoulder.
Her phone showed three o'clock exactly, the screen too bright in the country darkness.
From the corner came three soft knocks, unhurried, almost polite.
A long pause followed, measured, as though something were counting her heartbeats.
Then three more knocks, slightly softer, the way a question is repeated to someone who might not have heard.
Afterwards the silence returned so completely that she almost doubted the whole thing.
Her parents received the story with the special patience adults keep for children in new houses.
Nothing had been heard from their bedroom, and the house had been inspected before the purchase.
"Old wood contracts at night," her mother said. "It sounds like knocking, but it is only the cold."
It was a reasonable theory, and Mara genuinely tried to live inside it.
But the cold does not knock in threes, pause, and then ask again more gently.
By the end of the week she was sleeping with the lamp on, and the sounds had not missed a single night.
She started going to bed early, hoping to sleep through the appointment, and never once managed it.
On Saturday her father attacked the little lock with his toolbox and his pride.
The door surrendered after an hour, opening on a low space that smelled of dust and closed years.
They pulled out yellowed newspapers, a family of children's books, and a wooden box of photographs of strangers.
At the very back, wrapped in a piece of grey cloth, lay a pocket watch.
It had stopped decades ago; its delicate hands were frozen at exactly three o'clock.
"There is your ghost," her father announced, delighted. "A dead watch and a pile of paper."
Curiosity outlived the fear, and Mara spent Sunday going through the rescued papers.
The newspapers were fifty years old, full of markets, weather, and other people's weddings.
Inside one of the children's books she found a name written in careful ink: Elias Grey.
The same name appeared on a folded repair bill, under the words "watchmaker, main street".
A watchmaker had lived in this house, and this had almost certainly been his room.
She imagined him working at night by the window, surrounded by small patient machines.
A man like that, she thought, would wind his favorite watch every evening of his life.
And a watch like that, left alone in the dark, might reasonably knock to be remembered.
Mara cleaned the watch until the silver case showed her own face, small and serious.
She placed it on her table by the window, where it caught the last light of the evening.
There was something in its stillness that reminded her of a held breath.
That night she woke at three without surprise, as if keeping an appointment.
The knocks came from the corner, patient as ever, though the little door now stood open and empty.
She looked toward her table and felt the room grow colder: the watch was gone.
Downstairs, the house slept on, honestly unaware, the way houses always are.
Crossing the room took her longer than any distance she had ever walked.
She knelt before the open little door and looked inside with her small light.
The pocket watch lay in its exact old place, as though it had never been moved.
And it was running, filling the low space with a soft, steady tik, tik, tik.
It occurred to her that three o'clock might simply be the hour when everything had stopped.
Mara listened until her fear turned into something quieter and stranger, close to sympathy.
"All right," she whispered. "I understand now. This is your home, not mine."
She closed the little door as gently as one closes the cover of a finished book.
The knocking ended that night and has never returned.
The house feels different now, the way a room feels when an old argument has finally been settled.
Mara sleeps with the lamp off, and her parents believe the whole matter was imagination outgrown.
She lets them believe it, because some agreements are private.
She never told them about Elias Grey, or about the repair bill folded in her drawer.
But sometimes, at exactly three in the morning, a single contented tik rises from the corner.
And Mara, without opening her eyes, answers: "Good night."

quiz.q: What detail suggested the father was not fully calm about the little door?
options: He tried the lock twice before leaving
He refused to enter Mara's room
He called the previous owner that night

quiz.q: Why did the 'cold wood' theory fail for Mara?
options: The cold does not knock in a polite, repeated pattern
The house was warm at night
Her parents heard the knocking too

quiz.q: How did Mara's fear change at the end?
options: It turned into something close to sympathy
It grew until the family left the house
It disappeared when the watch was sold
